# Transcrição: 01-Why You Need Attribution Analysis To Optimise & Scale 

- **Fonte:** 01-Why You Need Attribution Analysis To Optimise & Scale 
- **Duração:** 24:25
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

I've been working in marketing since the early 2000s and attribution has been a problem since then, regardless of what's happened over time. Attribution has always been difficult. In fact, if you go back to the early 1900s, there was a book called Scientific Advertising, a really good book for anyone in marketing to read. And Claude Hopkins talks about attribution tracking for someone that reads a magazine and goes into store. So the solution was to have coupon codes and then to have different numbers on those coupon codes. So you could track which magazine that person came from and then based on redemption, what they purchased and tying that journey back. So you could have an idea of what's happening with your marketing, so which distribution platforms are working for you, and therefore make a better decision on where to place your marketing spend. So now if you fast forward to now and all the trouble we're having with trying to understand attribution and it's becoming more and more of a kind of focal area, the problem is the same. So the solution has to be based on what's available to us. So right now in the modern digital age, there's nothing stopping us from using coupon codes. And for anyone in Shopify, it's super simple to set up. You can have a coupon code based on many other things. So it could be top of funnel, mid funnel, bottom of funnel, if you're advertising, it could be for Facebook versus Google versus YouTube, it could be offline versus offline. So there's different ways to use that. Now for some brands, it's not even possible to use discount codes. So you need us some other way of tracking. And that's where analytics platforms come in. So the most used platform is Google Analytics. You can look at other platforms as well. When you look at your advertising, you're talking about Facebook and Google and YouTube, their attribution models, I would say, are selfish. So they only look at the data they've got. So Facebook only really looks at Facebook data. Google only really looks at Google data. But in the grand scheme of things, people, when they're buying a product, when they're searching, they'll use different platforms. So what I wanted to do is break that down in the simplest way I think I could. And when I try to explain things, I try and do it in a way that makes sense to me. So if it's oversimplified and you get it, that's because that's how I understand it as well. So I want to make it as simple as possible. Now these five attribution models I took from Google. So if you go into Google Analytics right now, you go into the attribution area and you try and look at your results and marketing attribution, these are the five models that Google presents. So last click should be obvious to everyone. So it's the last click that generated a sale or a sign up or whatever action you wanted. First click is the first click that generated the conversion, but based on a time window. So when you're looking at first click, there's always that impact of, is it 28

## [03:03 - 06:07]

day? Is it seven day? Is it one day? So just bear that in mind with first click. Linear is saying, you know, it doesn't matter where the sale came in. Everyone's going to get the same amount of reward. And I'll come to that as well. Position based says, well, this platform started the journey and this platform finished it or this click or whatever, you know, impression. So it's the first and the last click that gets the reward. And then time decay says, yes, the first click may have contributed, but as time goes on, it had less and less impact on the overall conversion. And you could argue that without the first click or the first impression, maybe nothing would have come to it. And I think this is the problem with attribution is there's no one size fits all. There's no way you can come into this and say, my business absolutely needs to use this model. And I'll explain why if I can just grab my drawing device based on a game of football and for anyone in North America, it's soccer, everyone else, it's football is actually football. So the way I see is this, when you have a team, when you have a team game like this and bear in mind, for any kind of marketing, it is literally a team game because you've got multiple players that are contributing to what you're doing. Now, in some cases, you're advertising, let's say, a low ticket product and you've only got one platform, then essentially in that case, you've got a team of one. So in that case, you've got this guy here who scores the goal. And it's very easy to attribute back and say, hey, well, actually, that guy scored the goal. It is both the first click and the last click. And therefore, the model is easy. So you know exactly what's going on and what to do with the data. But in reality, it doesn't really happen that way. So if we look at a, if we look at the kind of, a kind of more extended path. So let's say we start off here with the goalkeeper. So the goalkeeper has the ball here. He's now passing it out to this guy. He's now passing it out to this guy. And he's passed it to the striker. Now the striker here scores the goal. So in the last click model, it's very easy to say, right, this guy scored the goal. So therefore, as far as the last click goes, the attribution goes there. And in any kind of game of football, that's exactly how it goes. So when you look at the score, you see that this guy here scored the goal because he got the last touch, essentially. But is that fair on the other people that contributed? So actually, the first click, it could be first touch, actually came from this guy. So he started the move. Maybe the ball went out of play. He took the ball and started to pass it forward. And these ones are contributors. So first click all the way down the funnel, contribution, contribution, and then the last click and then the goal. Now, what I try and focus on is what is the daisy chain? The daisy

## [06:07 - 09:13]

chain is the path to purchase. Now, if you model this for every single conversion and you can identify the daisy chain, that's where the money's made. So if I know, for example, that my most lucrative move in my football team is that when my goalkeeper gets the ball, he passes specifically to this person, that person passes specifically to that person. And this person passes it to our star striker and we score a goal, then I'm going to try and replicate that as much as possible. So rather than saying, look, I recognize that my star striker, my bottom of funnel ad or whatever you're looking at is your last click. I know that works. But I want to understand the daisy chain. And this is the biggest challenge that I think we have with advertising is to understand the daisy chain. So, you know, if we talk about different advertising strategies and we talk about top of funnel video views, we talk about middle funnel, maybe get people into your website and then bottom of funnel to try and get them to convert, that's the daisy chain. It's exactly the same problem that we're trying to solve is do I really know my video views are working? I know in theory that if I run video views here, then, you know, it's going to have a good impact on giving me a video view audience. And then from there, I can market them with user generate content and then maybe an offer at the end of it. The problem is knowing, for example, which exact video is working or our videos in general working. And they're the problems that we're trying to solve with attribution. Now, if we go back to our model, we've talked about, let's get my pointy. So we talked about last click, which is who scored the goal or, you know, who generated the conversion, talk about first click. Now, if we talk about linear, in a linear kind of model, what you're saying is I don't really care which one converted, I just want fair attribution across them all. So in this kind of model, that's where you're looking for the daisy chain. So when you're looking at marketing attribution, there's different purposes of what you're doing. When you're trying to attribute, right, which channel is performing, now I'll give you an example, if you're an agency and you're, let's say running Facebook and Google and somebody else is running Pinterest or maybe they're doing internally, this becomes a challenge, right? Because you want to show your client that you have generated the cell. So you're fighting and scrambling over last click. If you want to grow and you want to do more top of funnel, you want to spend more, often you need to analyze that first click. But where linear really helps is to understand that daisy chain. Because now I want to know, I don't really care where the conversion happened or where the first click was, what's the most popular, what are the most popular linear paths? So in that instance, if I go into my Google Analytics account, and or into, you know, even with Facebook attribution, I think they're looking to bring it back at some point as well. If I can identify that certain clicks are happening, and this is something I'll cover off in a separate session. But with Google Analytics, you can add your UTM tracking,

## [09:13 - 12:15]

so you can track campaign, ad set and ad. And with Google Analytics, you can create a linear model. So you can actually look at where that click data is passing in from, where Google is capturing. So that's the second, the third part of capturing the daisy chain. And for me, this is one of the most important parts. So for reporting and analysis, last click and first click are useful. But linear is where you're really going to dial in and really try and understand how you can replicate what's working well. Now position based says, I don't really care about the part in between. I really care about, where did that journey start? And where did it end? Now, for example, you might have a situation where, you know, you're advertising on, let's say, Facebook doing video views. And the last click generally comes through Google branded search terms. Now, from your perspective, you don't want to expend too much energy and time trying to understand the inner details. All you know is when I run top of funnel activity on Facebook, and then I go and look at my data, I can see that, you know, Google is converting. And that's fine. So in that kind of instance, what you would often do is to track your EROAS. So if you don't know EROAS by now, you should. And it's something I've written quite a lot about. But with EROAS, what you're doing is doing an estimate of what your performance is. So there's estimated return on ad spend, and then estimated cost per acquisition. So essentially, what I'm doing with these is I'm saying, right, I don't really know what my actual ROAS or my actual CPA is, but I'm going to estimate it. So an estimate might say, let's say I'm looking at seven days data. So I know that on Facebook, I spent a certain amount on Google, I spent a certain amount. And then I know that I've generated X number of conversions. So what I'm doing is I'm looking at that data and saying, right, how many conversions did I generate? How much revenue did I generate overall? And then how much did I spend on my marketing? So that ratio becomes your EROAS. And then your eCPA is, you know, however many conversions are generated, divided by the spend you put in. Although it's not accurate, what that's telling you is, are these advertising channels and the activity I'm applying working to get me profitable results? So this isn't about EROAS and eCPA is not about giving you 100% accurate data that helps you to, you know, modify your marketing. What it's doing is it's trying to use parts of this to give you an idea of where to continue putting your money in. Now, not being 100% accurate, obviously, is a challenge because if something goes wrong in your daisy chain, how do you know what to analyze? And that's a completely different

## [12:15 - 15:17]

part of analysis. But essentially with estimated ROAS and estimate CPA, I'm taking my overall paid spend and my overall goals, and I'm checking to see how positive they are. So I'll give you an example. We have a client right now, they consistently run on a ROAS on Facebook of about 100, 120%. But on EROAS, they're consistently running on 500% or something like that, because, A, there's data that doesn't get tracked by Facebook. So, you know, we all know there's some actually tracking problems right now. But also, with the reduction in time windows, for example, on Facebook, where they've just gone from 28 day clip down to seven day, it's a product that we know from past analysis takes around two weeks for a customer to convert. It's an e-commerce product. So because we know that from past history, we can continue using EROAS and they do. And, you know, they could be spending 10, 20K a day, but they're doing it comfortably knowing that although the front end ROAS that we look at in the ads tool isn't as good as we'd want it to be, we know that the back end is still producing sales. Now, it's not a great solution. I'm not saying, you know, you should try not to plug the gaps and just use this high level data. But what I am saying is, we are able to scale consistently understanding this whole daisy chain method and understanding EROAS and ECPA. So, you know, once you've put that in place, once you've modeled it and you understand it and you understand, for example, we know that Facebook hovers at 100 to 150% ROAS. We know that Google most of the activities bottom of funnel that hovers at 250, 300%. But between them, our EROAS is amazingly good because also between that we have email and other things that contribute. And so, you know, in that kind of example where you have Facebook is essentially driving a lot of the traffic and then people are signing up to the offer, they might then see another email and then finally convert through Google. So that is now the daisy chain. And we are actually, this is one of the core daisy chains of this particular brand that we absolutely know work. So we, for example, if we look at our email attribution, I think about 40% of sales come through email. And that's great, but you can't really scale up email because you need emails to do so. And those emails are generated mostly from Facebook. So we know this daisy chain works and we're doing everything to amplify, optimize it and do better to eventually push out those bigger numbers. Now, this is a high seven figure brand. If, and you know, going right back to the first example, if you're just starting out, if you're generating $100 a day or $1,000 a day, you're still at the early stage where your daisy chain probably isn't that complex. Now, if you go right to the start of starting something new and you've just started on Facebook or e-commerce or any kind of product, more than likely your advertising starts here and it ends here. So

## [15:17 - 18:19]

you're essentially more focused on last click and you don't really care about the other models. So, you know, what I would say is it, it can get quite complex when you come into this and you think, well, my brand's not really that, my brand is quite small or my business is quite small. I'm not really sure what to do. Just focus on last click. That's all that matters to start off with. As you grow, then start looking at first click, then start looking at linear and then take it from there. Now, position-based, that can come as you start to grow and start to understand what's working top of funnel because what position-based allows you to do is I understand something's working top of funnel and bottom of funnel. If I can continue amplifying it, put more of spending, can I see those results come in and what are my benchmarks I'm putting in? So, this is the position-based model where I know that Facebook and Google, I want them to be attributed to the sale because that's the daisy chain that's working. So, if you, if you went into Google Analytics and look at position-based, it would say that, let's say for example, this sale generated $100, it would attribute $50 to Google and $50 to Facebook. If you went into a linear model, and this is where it does not work for your reporting, if you generate $100 of revenue as your goal, it would attribute $100 here, $100 here, $100 here and $100 here. Now, obviously that's wrong because you did not generate $400 worth of revenue. So, that's where linear can't really be used for your reporting. You can't go to a client and say, you know, we generate $400 and they look at the back end and say, well, there's only one sale and it was $100. How did that work? So, linear is used more for analysis than reporting. Last click, obviously, is the most common one. First click isn't really used for reporting. So, for example, if you generate $100 sale here, your first click would report as $100 and then zero down here. That's not, again, ideal for reporting purposes. Then you have position-based, which says, as I said before, you know, half is attributed there and half is attributed there. And then you have a time decay that says, you know, based on, and this is actually a really complex model, it says that we're going to attribute most of it to the last click, but then we'll generate a percentage for every other part of that daisy chain. So, it might be, you know, let's say $50 is attributed here and then $20 here and then $10, $20 here and then $10 there. So, that gives a fair representative of what's contributed, but it isn't still quite accurate. But essentially what you're trying to do is find a model that makes sense to the type of business you're running. Now, I mentioned e-commerce. Now, if I look at, for example, selling coaching. So, we talk about e-commerce funnels and e-commerce generally have a shorter path to purchase. Now, if I take an

## [18:19 - 21:20]

extended view of, let's say, a coaching funnel, so I run ads for my own coaching funnel and I'm able to see data in an extended view. Now, one thing that's interesting for me is the time decay model. So, I have a particular funnel and a daisy chain that I know that when someone comes through a particular organic channel, what actually happens in terms of contribution to ultimately becoming a client or a customer. So, by doing that, the time decay model allows me to attribute some impact to that original source. So, although I wouldn't give anywhere near as much attribution as the last click or parts between that because this alone doesn't work. This is one of the important things about first click. If you go to the first click model, you're saying that the first click really, really generated an immense amount of value. If you look at a 12-month funnel, like the one I'm explaining, that first click didn't have an immense value because it couldn't exist in terms of the conversion on its own. So, I know when someone joins my email list through this organic channel, they do a certain number of activities. They do a certain number of emails that they opened and some other identifiable things throughout. And then at the end of it, we might hit them up with an ad or something like that. So, as far as the ad platforms are concerned, they kind of join later on in the journey. And then it takes someone about four weeks to convert once they've seen an ad and they've clicked through, etc. So, if all I was doing was optimizing for my ads, I would miss out on a lot of my attribution. So, I wouldn't really understand where that original lead came from. So, for example, for this particular funnel, we have some warm campaigns running. So, warm being has engaged but not clicked. In this case, it's on the email list. And we know that when we've got them into this stage and they're warmed up, then when we start serving ads, then it becomes profitable. So, our Facebook row has looks great. But the problem is, could this exist in isolation on its own? It can't because it started off with this daisy chain. I can't just launch a Walmart because a Walmart doesn't exist as far as the funnel goes that we create because Walmart is someone that's engaged in a video, engaged in my page or on my email list, etc. That has to have come from somewhere. That somewhere is this path here. And I know that this path is slightly extended. So, I can't really scale it up and see immediate impact. But I can scale it up here being fairly confident that I understand the daisy chain and it will pay back. Obviously, this is an extremely example of a 12-month funnel. But not all funnels need to be like that. But this is how, for example, the time decay model works. I don't personally use it for e-commerce because it's too short. Even in the case of a two-week conversion path for e-commerce, it's still the time decay model is too short. So, I don't really need to attribute something so much in that kind

## [21:20 - 24:22]

of instance. But what I want to do is play around with models and be able to answer specific questions. So, if you go back to this model, last click says, what's actually generating the sale? So, this is all about bottom of funnel at the moment. First click says, where is my awareness coming from? Where's my first click or my first impression coming from? Because when you just look at the last click model, very rarely will you be able to scale it up. You can't scale up your hot campaigns, your site retargeting. You just can't because there are a limited number of people and you can max your budgets out but then your frequency goes up and then your performance drops. So, if last click is coming from your bottom of funnel, you then need to identify what's working top of funnel. Then, if I want to have a look at what my daisy chain is, so which paths are actually working, then I'd use that linear model. So, now the question I want answered is nothing to do with reporting and things like that. I just want every channel that contributed to be rewarded with recognition. So, in the game of football, for example, what would end up happening is, you know, your team might have won 1-0 but these four players were acknowledged as contributing to the goal. So, in a game of football, for example, you have the last click or the last kick, which is the person that scored the goal and then it says this person assisted. So, if anyone follows football, often when someone scores a goal, they say this person assisted. The problem with that is how valuable was that assist? Now, you know, because I follow football quite a lot, they've now created a new metric which is called the pre-assist. So, this person gave the pre-assist but this person gave the assist. But the thing is, it's still not accurate because if you look at that game of football and, you know, your star striker scored and your star midfielder passed the ball, but this guy actually, you know, took it past two, three players and really cleared up the field. So, all this guy had to do was pass it and then this guy scored. It's difficult to judge. So, if you're into sports science, this goes for every team sport, they're always analyzing what is the path. You know, you can look at American football, hockey, they're always looking for these paths to understand what's working, what can be tweaked, etc. And the same thing applies to your advertising. You want to identify which of those players in your funnel are giving the best contribution and therefore what you can amp up. And actually, you know, conversely, what's not working because you got all these players here, what if, you know, some of these players are just not contributing. Let's say, for example, you do your analysis and you find that this guy here, he's just, or he or she, obviously, a game of football, but this person here is just not contributing or sometimes when the ball is played there, that person just does not contribute. It just, you know, it just disappears. And this, again, the same thing happens to your marketing. You're going to find ads that not performing ad sets, campaigns, platforms, it's all about understanding what's working and

## [24:22 - 24:25]

contributing to your overall goal.
